Google 


This  is  a  digital  copy  of  a  book  that  was  preserved  for  generations  on  library  shelves  before  it  was  carefully  scanned  by  Google  as  part  of  a  project 

to  make  the  world's  books  discoverable  online. 

It  has  survived  long  enough  for  the  copyright  to  expire  and  the  book  to  enter  the  public  domain.  A  public  domain  book  is  one  that  was  never  subject 

to  copyright  or  whose  legal  copyright  term  has  expired.  Whether  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  may  vary  country  to  country.  Public  domain  books 

are  our  gateways  to  the  past,  representing  a  wealth  of  history,  culture  and  knowledge  that's  often  difficult  to  discover. 

Marks,  notations  and  other  maiginalia  present  in  the  original  volume  will  appear  in  this  file  -  a  reminder  of  this  book's  long  journey  from  the 

publisher  to  a  library  and  finally  to  you. 

Usage  guidelines 

Google  is  proud  to  partner  with  libraries  to  digitize  public  domain  materials  and  make  them  widely  accessible.  Public  domain  books  belong  to  the 
public  and  we  are  merely  their  custodians.  Nevertheless,  this  work  is  expensive,  so  in  order  to  keep  providing  tliis  resource,  we  liave  taken  steps  to 
prevent  abuse  by  commercial  parties,  including  placing  technical  restrictions  on  automated  querying. 
We  also  ask  that  you: 

+  Make  non-commercial  use  of  the  files  We  designed  Google  Book  Search  for  use  by  individuals,  and  we  request  that  you  use  these  files  for 
personal,  non-commercial  purposes. 

+  Refrain  fivm  automated  querying  Do  not  send  automated  queries  of  any  sort  to  Google's  system:  If  you  are  conducting  research  on  machine 
translation,  optical  character  recognition  or  other  areas  where  access  to  a  large  amount  of  text  is  helpful,  please  contact  us.  We  encourage  the 
use  of  public  domain  materials  for  these  purposes  and  may  be  able  to  help. 

+  Maintain  attributionTht  GoogXt  "watermark"  you  see  on  each  file  is  essential  for  in  forming  people  about  this  project  and  helping  them  find 
additional  materials  through  Google  Book  Search.  Please  do  not  remove  it. 

+  Keep  it  legal  Whatever  your  use,  remember  that  you  are  responsible  for  ensuring  that  what  you  are  doing  is  legal.  Do  not  assume  that  just 
because  we  believe  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  the  United  States,  that  the  work  is  also  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  other 
countries.  Whether  a  book  is  still  in  copyright  varies  from  country  to  country,  and  we  can't  offer  guidance  on  whether  any  specific  use  of 
any  specific  book  is  allowed.  Please  do  not  assume  that  a  book's  appearance  in  Google  Book  Search  means  it  can  be  used  in  any  manner 
anywhere  in  the  world.  Copyright  infringement  liabili^  can  be  quite  severe. 

About  Google  Book  Search 

Google's  mission  is  to  organize  the  world's  information  and  to  make  it  universally  accessible  and  useful.   Google  Book  Search  helps  readers 
discover  the  world's  books  while  helping  authors  and  publishers  reach  new  audiences.  You  can  search  through  the  full  text  of  this  book  on  the  web

'  I  'UU  Catalogue  of  Copyright  Entries  is  published  in  four  parts.    Part  1,  Group  2, 

1  Issued  monthly,  contains  mainlr  the  titles  of  pomphleta,  leafiets,  and  Uterar; 
contributloas  to  periodicals,  and  all  other  productions  registered  under  the  legal 
designation  "book"  not  found  in  Group  1  (issued  sereral  times  a  week),  including 
kIso,  howerer,  preliminai;  reports  of  court  decisions,  local  directories,  herdbooks, 
and  other  inntMto,  etc.  The  entries  are  arranged  alphabeticBllr  hj  authors,  or,  if 
anonymous,  by  tilb.  An  asterisk  (*j  alter  the  author's  name  Indicates  that  the 
author  and  cop^ight  proprietor  are  the  same.  When  the  author  and  copyright  pro- 
prietoi  are  dUUnot,  a  separate  statement  is  made  at  the  end  of  the  title,  giving  the 
profriator  and  his  address. 

Gfoup  2  also  CMitains,  in  three  separate  alphabets,  the  titles  gf  the  entries  mada 
in  Class  "C,"  Lectures,  sermons,  addresses,  ^vpared  for  oral  delivery,  which  are 
arranged  alphabetically  by  author;  Class  "D,"  Dramatic  compositions,  arranged 
alphabetically  by  title;  and  Class  "F,"  Haps,  arranged  alphabetically  by  the  subject 
word  of  each  title. 

Under  the  act  of  March  4,  1909,  in  effect  bom  July  1,  1909,  the  preliminary  entry 
of  titles  has  been  abrogated  and  registration  follows  deposit  of  copies  with  an  appli- 
cation for  entry  in  the  Copyright  Office. 

To  aa?e  space  in  the  Catalogue,  the  copyright  notice  after  each  title  is  abbreiiated. 
The  abbreviations  used  in  this  part  are  as  follows:  "A,"  Class  A,  Books;  "A — Foreign," 
Foreign  books  in  fore^  lat^u^es;  "C,"  Class  C,  Lectures,  sermons,  addresses, 
prepared  for  oral  delivery;  "D,"  Class  D,  Dramatic  compositions;  "F,"  Class  F, 
Haps.  "©"  implies  copyright  registration  of  a  published  work,  followed  by  date 
of  publication;  "©  1  c."  stands  for  copyright  of  an  unpublished  work,  followed  by- 
date  of  receipt  in  the  Copyright  Office  of  the  printed  or  manuscript  copy;  and  "2  c." 
for  two  copies,  followed  by  date  of  their  receipt.  In  the  case  of  contributions  to 
periodicals  only  one  copy  of  the  issue  or  issues  containing  such  contribution  is  required . 
"A  ad  int."  implies  ad  interim  copyright  registration  under  the  law  of  Harch  4,  1909> 
of  a  book  published  abroad  in  the  English  language,  for  which  the  deposit  of  only 
one  copy  is  required. 

Alistof  the  books  ofwhich  the  subsisting  copyright  has  been  renewed  and  extended 
under  section  24  of  the  act  of  Harch  4,  1909,  will  be  found  in  Part  1,  Group  1. 

Each  number  contains  an  index  of  the  names  of  all  copyright  proprietors,  the  names 
of  well-known  authors  of  pamphlets,  occasionally  the  titles  of  anonymous  pamphlets, 
and  the  names  of  all  autliors  of  dramatic  compositions.  The  annual  index,  furnished 
with  the  December  issue,  supplies,  in  addition,  the  titles  of  all  dramas  and  the  sub- 
ject words  of  all  maps. 

The  eimual  subscription  price  for  the  Catalogue  of  Copyright  Entries  (received 
only  for  the  calendar  year)  for  Part  1,  Groups  1  and  2,  is  Sl.OO  for  1913,  payable  in 
advance  to  the  Superintendent  of  Documents,  Washington,  D.  C,  by  postal  money 
order,  express  order,  or  New  Tork  draft. 

The  act  of  March  4, 1909,  provides  that  the  Catalogue  irf  Copyright 
Entries  "  shall  be  admitted  in  any  court  as  priuui  uci£  evidence  of 
the  facts  stated  therein  as  regards  any  copyr^^t  registraiion."

"pHE  Catalogue  of  CopTilght  Entries  is  published  is  foui  parts.  Part  1,  Gnwf  2, 
^  issued  monthly,  cantains  mainly  tiie  titles  of  pamphlets,  leaflets,  and  litetiij 
cmtribntians  to  periodicals,  and  ill  o&w  productiona  registered  under  the  1^1 
designation  "book"  not  found  in  Group  I  (issned  sereral  timea  a  week),  including 
also,  howerer,  preliminaiy  repeats  of  court  deciaiona,  local  directories,  herdbcoka, 
and  other  annuals,  etc.  The  entries  are  arranged  alphabetically  by  authws,  ot,  if 
anonymous,  by  title.  An  asterisk  <*)  after  the  author's  name  indicates  that  die 
author  and  copyright  proprietor  are  the  same.  Then  the  author  and  copyr^t  pro- 
prietor are  dlfFerent,  a  separate  statement  is  made  at  the  end  of  the  title,  giving  the 
proprietor  and  his  addivsa. 

Group  2  also  contains,  In  three  separate  alphabets,  ttie  titles  of  the  entries  made 
in  Class  "C,"  Lectures,  sermons,  addresses,  prepared  for  oral  delivery,  which  on 
arranged  alphabetically  by  author;  Class  "D,"  Dramatic  compositions,  amnged 
alphabetically  by  title;  and  Class  "F,"  Haps,  airangad  alphabetically  by  the  subject 
wwd  of  each  title. 

Dnder  (he  act  of  March  4,  190S>,  in  effect  from  July  1,  1900,  the  prelimloacy  eutiT 
of  titles  has  been  abrogated  and  registration  follows  deposit  of  copies  with  an  appli- 
cation for  entry  in  the  Copyright  Office. 

To  save  space  in  the  Catalogue,  the  copyright  notice  after  each  title  is  abbreviated. 
The  abbreviations  used  in  this  part  are  as  fallows;  "A,"  Class  A,  Books;  "A— Foreign," 
Foreign  books  in  foreign  languages,  "C,"  Class  C,  Lectures,  sermons,  addresses, 
prepared  for  oral  delivery;  "D,"  Class  D,  Dramatic  compoaitions;  "F,"  Class  I, 
Haps.  "©"  implies  copyr^t  registration  of  a  published  work,  followed  by  date 
of  publication;  "©  1  c."  stands  Ibr  copyright  of  on  unpublished  work,  followed  by 
date  of  receipt  in  tlie  Copyright  Office  of  the  printed  or  manuscript  copy;  and  "2  c." 
for  two  copies,  followed  by  date  of  their  receipt.  In  the  case  of  contributioDS  to 
periodicals  only  one  copy  of  the  issue  or  issues  containing  such  contribution  is  required. 
"A  ad  int."  implies  ad  interim  copyright  registration  under  the  law  of  Harch  4,  1909, 
of  a  book  published  abroad  in  the  English  language,  for  which  the  deposit  of  oo^ 
one  copy  is  required. 

A  list  of  the  books  of  which  the  subsisting  copyright  has  been  renewed  and  extended 
under  section  24  of  the  act  of  Harch  4,  1909,  will  be  found  in  Part  1,  Group  1. 

Each  nnmber  contains  an  index  of  the  names  of  all  copyright  proprietors,  the  names 
of  well-known  authors  of  pamphlets,  occasionally  the  titles  of  anonymous  pamphlets, 
and  the  names  of  all  authors  of  dramatic  compositions.  The  annual  index,  furnished 
with  the  December  issue,  supplies.  In  addition,  the  titles  of  all  dramas  and  the  sub- 
ject words  of  all  maps. 

The  annual  subscription  price  for  the  Catalogue  of  Copyright  Entries  (received 
only  for  tbe  calendar  year)  for  Part  1,  Groups  1  and  2,  is  ^l-OO  for  1913,  payable  in 
advance  to  the  Superintendent  of  Documents,  Washington,  D.  C,  by  postal  mmiey 
order,  express  order,  or  New  York  draft. 

The  act  of  March  4, 1909,  provides  that  the  Oatalo^e  of  Copyright 
Entries  "  shall  be  admitted  in  any  court  as  prima  facie  evidence  of 
the  facts  stated  therein  as  regards  any  copyright  registration."

■  I  'HM  Catalogue  of  Copn^t  Entarlea  is  publielied  is  fdui  parts.    Part  1,  Gmtp  2, 

i  Issued  moafblj,  contalna  mainly  tlie  titles  of  pamphlets,  leaflets,  and  litaatr 
Gontributioiis  to  periodicals,  and  all  other  prodnctioas  legisterad  uuder  the  I^ 
designatioit  "book"  not  found  In  Group  1  (issued  several  times  a  week),  including 
also,  howeTcs',  preliatinary  reports  of  court  decisions,  local  dliectoiies,  hccdbooks, 
end  other  annuals,  etc.  The  entries  are  arrai^ed  alphabeticallj  by  authors,  or,  if 
anonymous,  by  title.  An  asterisk  (*)  after  the  aathcc's  name  indicates  that  the 
author  and  copyright  proprietor  are  the  same.  When  the  antkor  and  ct^yright  pro> 
prietoi  are  different,  a  separate  statement  Is  made  at  tlie  end  of  the  title,  giring  the 
pro^etoi  and  hia  address. 

Oroup  2  also  contains,  in  thiee  separate  alphabets,  the  titles  of  the  entries  made 
in  Class  "C,"  Lectures,  sermons,  addresses,  prepared  for  oral  deliTety,  which  are 
arm^ed  alphsbetlcaily  by  author;  Class  "D,"  Dramatic  compositioiis,  ananged 
alphabetically  by  title;  and  Class  "F,"  Haps,  arranged  alphabetically  by  the  subject 
word  of  each  title. 

Under  the  act  of  Harch  4,  1900,  in  effect  from  July  1,  1900,  the  preliminary  entry 
of  titles  has  been  abrogated  and  registration  follows  deposit  of  copies  with  an  aff^ 
cation  for  entry  in  the  Copyright  OfCLce. 

To  sa*e  apace  in  the  Catalogue,  the  copyr^t  notice  after  each  title  is  abbieviated. 
The  abbreviations  used  In  this  part  are  as  follows:  "A,"  Class  A,  Books;  "A — Foreign," 
Foreign  books  in  foreign  languages;  "C,"  Class  C,  Lectures,  sermons,  addresses, 
prepared  foe  oral^  dellTery;  "D,"  Class  D,  Dramatic  compositions;  "F,"  Class  F, 
Haps.  "©".Implies  co^righl  reglstiation  of  a  published  work,  followed  by  date 
of  publication;  "©  1  c."  stands  for  copyright  of  an  ui^ublished  work,  followed  by 
date  of  receipt  In  the  Copyright  Office  of  the  printed  or  manuscript  cof^;  and  "2  c" 
for  two  copies,  followed  by  date  of  their  receipt.  In  the  case  of  contributions  to 
periodicals  only  one  copy  of  the  issue  <a  issues  containing  such  contribution  is  required. 
"A  ad  int."  implies  ed  interim  copyr^t  registration  under  the  law  of  Harch  4,  IMP, 
of  a  book  published  abroad  In  the  English  langu^e,  for  which  the  deposit  of  enly 
one  copy  is  required. 

A  list  of  the  books  of  which  the  subsisting  copyright  has  been  renewed  and  extended 
under  section  24  of  the  act  of  Harch  4,  1909,  will  be  found  in  Part  I,  Group  1. 

Each  number  contains  an  index  of  the  names  of  all  copyright  proprietors,  the  names 
of  well-known  authors  of  pamphlets,  occasionally  the  titles  of  anonymous  pamfdilets, 
and  the  names  of  all  authors  of  dramatic  compositions.  The  annual  index,  fumiabed 
with  the  December  isaue,  suppliea,  in  addition,  the  titles  of  all  dramas  end  the  sub- 
ject wm'ds  of  all  mapa. 

The  annual  subscription  ^ce  tor  tiie  Catalogue  of  Copyright  Kitries  (received 
only  for  the  calendar  year)  for  Part  1,  Groups  1  and  2,  Is  fl.m  for  1913,  payable  in 
advance  to  the  Superintendent  of  Documents,  Washington,  D.  C,  by  postal  money 
order,  express  order,  or  Hew  York  draft. 

The  act  of  March  4, 1909,  provides  that  the  Catalogue  of  Copyright 
Entries  "  shall  be  admitted  in  any  court  as  prima  facie  evidence  of 
the  facts  stated  therein  as  regards  any  copyright  registration.."

n\HE  Catalogue  of  C<^riight  SntrloB  is  published  In  fbni  pirts.  Part  I,  Group  2, 
^  issued  monthlr,  coatains  mainly  the  titles  of  pamphlets,  leaflets,  and  literary 
contributions  to  periodicals,  and  all  other  productions  registered  under  the  legal 
designation  "book"  not  fotmd  in  Group  1  (issued  sCTeral  times  a  week),  incltiding 
also,  howerer,  prelimioai;  reports  of  court  decisions,  local  directories,  herdbooks, 
and  other  annuals,  etc.  The  entries  are  arranged  alphabetically  by  authors,  or,  if 
anonymona,  by  title.  An  asterisk  (*)  after  the  author's  name  Indicates  that  die 
author  and  ct^yright  proprietor  are  the  same.  When  the  author  and  copyright  pn»- 
prietor  are  difhrent,  a  separate  statemAnt  Is  made  at  the  end  of  the  title,  givlaj  the 
frofcietor  and  his  addrssa. 

Oioup  2  also  contUns,  in  three  separate  alphabets,  the  titles  of  the  entries  made 
in  Clasa  "C,"  Lectures,  aennons,  addresses,  jvepared  for  ocal  delivery,  which  are 
arranged  alphabetically  by  author;  Class  "D,"  Dramatic  compositioaa,  arranged 
alphabetically  by  title;  and  Class  "F,"  Haps,  arranged  alphabetically  by  the  subject 
word  of  each  title. 

Under  the  act  of  March  4,  1909,  in  effect  from  July  1,  1909,  the  preliminary  entry 
of  titles  has  been  abrogated  and  registration  follows  deposit  of  cc^ies  with  an  appli- 
cation for  entry  in  the  Copyright  Office. 

To  save  spece  in  the  Catalogue,  the  copyright  notice  after  each  title  is  abbreviated. 
The  abbreviations  uaed  in  this  part  are  as  follows:  "k,"  Class  A,  Books;  "A—Foreign," 
Foreign  books  in  foreign  languages;  "C,"  Class  C,  Lectures,  senuonB,  addresses, 
prepared  for  oral  delivery;  "D,"  Class  D,  Dramatic  compositions;  "F,"  Class  F, 
Haps.  "©"  implies  copyright  registration  of  a  publiahed  work,  fallowed  by  dale 
of  publication;  "©  1  c."  stands  for  copyright  of  an  unpublished  work,  followed  by 
date  of  receipt  in  the  Copyright  Office  of  the  printed  or  manuscript  copy;  and  "2  c." 
for  two  copies,  followed  by  date  of  their  receipt.  In  the  case  of  coatrihutionB  to 
periodicals  only  one  copy  of  the  issue  or  Issues  containing  such  contribution  is  required. 
"A  ad  int"  implies  ad  interim  copyright  registration  under  tbe  law  of  Harch  4,  1909, 
of  a  book  published  abroad  in  the  English  language,  for  which  the  deposit  of  only 
one  copy  is  required. 

Alist  of  the  books  of  which  the  subsisting  copyright  has  been  renewed  and  extended 
under  section  24  of  the  act  of  Harch  4,  1909,  will  be  found  in  Part  1,  Group  I. 

Each  number  contains  an  index  of  the  names  of  all  copyright  proprietors,  the  names 
of  well-known  authns  of  pamphlets,  occasionally  the  titlea  of  anonymous  pamphlets, 
and  the  names  of  all  authors  of  dramatic  compoaltions.  The  aimttal  index,  furnished 
with  the  December  issue,  supplies,  in  addition,  the  titles  of  all  dramas  and  the  sub- 
ject words  of  all  maps. 

The  aimual  aubscription  price  for  the  Catalogue  of  Ct^right  Entries  (received 
only  for  the  calendar  year)  fbr  Part  1,  Groups  1  and  2,  is  $1.00  for  1913,  payable  in 
advance  to  the  Superintendent  of  Documents,  Washington,  D.  C,  by  postal  money 
order,  express  order,  or  New  York  drsft. 

Tlie  act  of  March  4, 1909.  provides  that  the  Catalogue  of  Copyright 
Entries  "shall  be  admitted  iti  any  court  as  prima  facie  evidence  of 
the  facts  stated  therein  as  regards  any  copyright  registration."

TEE  Catalogue  of  Copft^t  Entries  is  published  in  four  parts.  Part  1,  Gn» 
issued  monthly,  contains  mainly  the  titles  of  pamphlets,  leaflets,  and  111 
Gontribntions  to  periodicals,  and  all  other  productions  registered  under  the 
designation  "book"  not  found  in  Group  1  (Issued  sereral  times  a  week),  incla 
also,  howoTer,  preliminary  reports  of  court  decisions,  local  directories,  herdb< 
and  other  annuals,  etc.  The  entries  are  arranged  alphabetically  by  authots,  \ 
anonymous,  by  title.  An  asterisk  (*)  after  the  author's  name  indicates  tbU 
author  and  copyright  proprietor  are  the  same.  When  the  author  and  c^;riglil 
piletor  are  diSeieiil,  a  separate  statement  Is  made  at  the  end  of  the  title,  girin) 
proprietor  aod  his  address. 

Group  2  also  contains,  in  three  separate  alphabets,  the  titles  of  the  entries  i 
In  Class  "C,"  Lectures,  sermons,  addresses,  prepared  lor  oral  delivery,  whid 
arranged  alphabetically  by  author;  Class  "D,"  Dramatic  compositions,  arra 
alphabetically  by  title;  and  Class  "F,"  Maps,  arranged  alphabetically  by  the  sul 
word  of  each  title. 

Dnder  the  act  of  March  4,  lOW,  in  effect  from  July  1,  1X9,  the  preliminary  i 
of  titles  has  been  abrogated  and  leglstration  follows  deposit  of  copies  with  an  ■ 
cation  for  entry  in  the  Co^right  Office. 

To  save  space  in  the  Catalogue,  the  ct^rrigU  notice  after  each  title  is  abbrerii 
The  abbreviations  used  in  this  part  are  as  follows:  "A,"  Class  A,  Books ;  "A— Forei, 
Foreign  books  in  foreign  lar^uages;  "C,"  Class  C,  Lectures,  sermons,  addrei 
prepared  for  oral  delivery;  "D,"  Class  D,  Dramatic  compositions;  "F,"  Clas 
Maps.  "©"  implies  copyright  r^lstratlon  of  a  published  work,  followed  by 
of  publication;  "©  I  c."  stands  for  copyright  of  an  unpublished  work,  follows 
date  of  receipt  in  the  Copyright  Office  of  the  printed  or  manuscript  copy;  and  "2 
&a  two  copies,  followed  by  date  of  their  receipt.  In  the  case  ol  cootribution 
periodicals  only  one  copy  of  the  issue  or  issues  containing  such  contribution  is  reqni 
"A  ad  Int."  implies  ad  interim  copyright  r^istiatioti  under  the  law  of  March  4,  1 
of  a  book  published  abroad  in  the  English  language,  for  which  the  deposit  of 
one  copy  Is  required. 

A  list  of  the  books  of  which  the  subsisting  copyright  has  been  renewed  and  eztei 
under  section  24  of  the  act  of  March  4,  1909,  will  be  found  in  Part  1,  Group  1. 

Eachnnmber  contains  an  index  of  the  names  of  all  c^yrightproitfietors,  thena 
of  well-known  authors  of  pamphlets,  occasionally  the  titles  of  anonymous  pan^ 
and  the  names  of  all  authors  of  dramatic  compositions.  The  armnal  index,  frimli 
with  the  December  issue,  supplies.  In  additioQ,  the  titles  of  all  dramas  and  the  i 
|ect  words  of  all  maps. 

The  annual  subscription  price  toi  the  Catalogue  of  C^yright  Entries  (rece 
only  for  the  calendar  year)  for  Part  1,  Groups  1  and  2,  is  |1.00  fat  1913,  payabi 
advance  to  the  Superintendent  of  Documents,  Washington,  D,  C,  by  postal  mi 
urder,  express  order,  or  Hew  York  draft. 

The  act  of  March  4, 1909,  provides  that  the  Catalogue  of  Copyri; 
Entries  "  shall  be  admitted  in  any  court  as  prima  facie  evidence 
the  facts  stated  therein  as  regards  any  copyright  registration."
